Early skyscrapers
By the 1890s , Chicago architects were producing a solution to this problem , creating a new architectural style , often termed the `` Chicago school of architecture '' . The school included architects such as Louis Sullivan , William Le Baron Jenney and John Root , whose designs combined architectural aesthetic theory with practical commercial sense . They favoured placing rich , ornate designs on the outside of skyscrapers at the ground level and simpler , plainer ornamentation on the upper levels , with strong vertical lines . The roofs of their skyscrapers typically formed a comprehensible outline and structure when seen at a distance as part of the city skyline . The intent was to draw the observer 's eye upwards , celebrating what Sullivan termed the `` lofty '' nature of the skyscraper , but not wasting resources on intricate detailing unlikely to appeal to a busy businessman . At the same time , the more lavish ground floor designs would make the building stand out to passers - by and pull in the necessary business for a successful commercial building . The

Great Lakes region
Chicago and Detroit carry important roles in the field of architecture . Chicago pioneered the world 's first skyscraper , the Home Insurance Building designed by William LeBaron Jenney . Engineering innovation established Chicago from that time on to become one of the world 's most influential epicenters of contemporary urban and commercial architecture . Equally influential was the 1832 invention of balloon - framing in Chicago which replaced heavy timber construction requiring massive beams and great woodworking skill with pre-cut timber . This new lumber could be nailed together by farmers and settlers who used it to build homes and barns throughout the western prairies and plains . Wisconsin - born , Chicago - trained Sullivan apprentice Frank Lloyd Wright designed prototypes for architectural designs from the commercial skylight atrium to suburban ranch house .

Early skyscrapers
Meanwhile , Chicago 's skyscraper industry also boomed during the decade before World War I . The city 's elevated train network was operating by 1910 , allowing more workers to come into the centre . In 1910 alone 1,500,000 square feet ( 140,000 m ) of new offices were opened and by the end of the decade , Chicago had the second largest number of headquarters offices in the U.S. Chicago architectural firms such as Daniel H. Burnham and then Graham , Anderson , Probst & White continued to design skyscrapers in the palazzo style made popular in the previous decade . Chicago had hosted the World 's Columbian Exposition in 1893 , a massive international event which had excited interest in the themes of classical architecture and well - designed city landscapes . Chicago also had extensive discussions in 1909 about the potential for designing large parts of the city , the Burnham Plan of Chicago .

input.text: What happens in a T Tauri phase?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: In the T Tauri phase, a star is surrounded by a dense disk of gas and dust. The star ejects extremely hot winds dominated by positively charged particles called protons and neutral helium atoms. While some of the material from the disk is still falling onto the star, small groups of dust particles are colliding and clumping together, growing into larger objects. These are the building blocks of planets, sometimes called "planetesimals."

Stars in an Exoplanet World - NASA Science
The bigger (or more massive) the star, the shorter its life, as more massive stars burn their nuclear fuel faster.This infographic compares the characteristics of three classes of stars in our galaxy: Sunlike stars are classified as G-type stars; stars less massive and cooler than our Sun are K dwarfs; and even fainter and cooler stars are the reddish M dwarfs. The habitable zones, potentially capable of hosting life-bearing planets, are wider for hotter stars. The longevity for red dwarf M stars can exceed 100 billion years. K dwarf ages can range from 15 to 45 billion years. Meanwhile, our Sun only lasts for 10 billion years. The relative amount of harmful radiation (to life as we know it) that stars emit can be 80 to 500 times more intense for M dwarfs relative to our Sun, but only 5 to 25 times more intense for the orange K dwarfs. Red dwarfs make up the bulk of the Milky Way's population, about 73%. Sunlike stars are merely 6% of the population, and K dwarfs are at 13%.NASA, ESA and Z. Levy (STScI)Download the infographicHow do planets form around stars?The gas and dust that swirl around a star during its formation are critical to forming planets around it. The dust contains heavy elements such as carbon and iron that form the cores of planets.Scientists think planets start off as grains of dust smaller than the width of a human hair. They emerge from the giant, donut-shaped disk of gas and dust that circles young stars. Gravity and other forces cause material within the disk to collide. If the collision is gentle enough, the material fuses, growing like rolling snowballs. Over time, dust particles combine to form pebbles, which evolve into mile-sized rocks. As these planetesimals, or planets in the making, orbit their star, they clear material from their path, leaving tracks of space empty but for fine dust. At the same time, the star gobbles up nearby gas while pushing more distant material farther away.

Stars in an Exoplanet World - NASA Science
Gravity and other forces cause material within the disk to collide. If the collision is gentle enough, the material fuses, growing like rolling snowballs. Over time, dust particles combine to form pebbles, which evolve into mile-sized rocks. As these planetesimals, or planets in the making, orbit their star, they clear material from their path, leaving tracks of space empty but for fine dust. At the same time, the star gobbles up nearby gas while pushing more distant material farther away. After a few million years, the disk will have totally transformed, much of it taking the form of new worlds.The life cycles of starsRed giants and white dwarfsWhen an average star like our Sun runs out of hydrogen to fuse, the star starts to collapse. But compacting a star causes it to heat up again and it is able to fuse what little hydrogen remains in a shell wrapped around its core. This burning shell of hydrogen greatly expands the outer layers of the star. When this happens, the star becomes a red giant. When our Sun enters the red giant phase of its life, in about 5 billion years, it will be so big that Mercury will be completely swallowed.Our red giant Sun will still be consuming helium and cranking out carbon. When the helium is gone, the Sun will succumb to gravity again. When the core contracts, it will cause a release of energy and the Sun will become an even bigger giant with a radius beyond Earth's orbit.U Camelopardalis, or U Cam for short, is a star nearing the end of its life. As stars run low on fuel, they become unstable. Every few thousand years, U Cam coughs out a nearly spherical shell of gas as a layer of helium around its core begins to fuse. The gas ejected in the star's is clearly visible in this picture as a faint bubble of gas surrounding the red giant star. ESA/NASAAfter about a billion years as a red giant, the Sun will have ejected its outer layers until, eventually, its stellar core is exposed. This dead (in terms of nuclear fusion) but still ferociously hot stellar cinder is called a white dwarf. White dwarfs are roughly the size of Earth, despite containing the mass of a star.

Stars in an Exoplanet World - NASA Science
Red dwarfs make up the bulk of the Milky Way's population, about 73%. Sunlike stars are merely 6% of the population, and K dwarfs are at 13%.NASA, ESA and Z. Levy (STScI)How are stars born?Stars are born from vast clouds of gas and dust, known as nebulae, that are scattered throughout most galaxies. Over thousands to millions of years, gravity can cause denser pockets within a nebula to collapse under their own weight. As a cloud – which is mostly hydrogen – collapses, the material at its center begins to heat up. Known as a “protostar,” this hot core of the collapsing cloud will is a star in the making. Some of these spinning clouds of collapsing gas and dust break up into two or three blobs that each form stars. This would explain why most of the stars in the Milky Way come in pairs or in multiples. Not all of this material ends up as part of the star, however – the remaining dust can become planets and moons, asteroids, and comets – or may simply remain as dust.What is a main sequence star?As millions of years pass, the core temperature of a protostar reaches a point at which nuclear fusion can begin. The star then begins the longest stage of its life, called the “main sequence.” Most stars in the galaxy, including our Sun, are categorized as main sequence. This is a state in which nuclear fusion in the star is stable and hydrogen is converted to helium. This process releases a lot of energy that keeps the star hot and bright, and it supplies an outward pressure against the incredible mass of material that would otherwise cause the star to collapse on itself. Ninety percent of a star’s life is spent in the main sequence phase.What does a star's color mean?When you look at the night sky, you may notice that some stars shine more brightly than others. The brightness of a star is related to how much energy it puts out, as well as how close it is to Earth.Stars also vary in color – because they vary in temperature. Hotter stars appear blue or white, while cooler stars look orange or red.

These variations in disk composition can affect the types of planets that form around the stars.

input.text: My React application using the App Configuration React client SDK is not connecting to the App Configuration service instance despite correct initialization. How can I get this fixed?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: Here are a few steps you can take to troubleshoot the issue: 1. **Check your internet connection**: Ensure that your React application has a stable internet connection, as the App Configuration React client SDK needs to connect to the App Configuration service instance. 2. **Verify the initialization parameters**: Double-check the region, guid, apikey, collectionId, and environmentId values you're using for initialization. Make sure they are correct and match the ones in your App Configuration service instance. 3. **Check if the App Configuration SDK handles the automatic delivery of the appropriate flag state or property value into your application from the App Configuration instance. 4. **Create service credentials for Client SDK**: Ensure that you have created the service credentials of the role Client SDK for use with the React client SDK. 5. **Test the connection**: Check if the various metrics are sent back to the AC cloud service and the service is operating properly so that you can monitor its operation. If you've tried these steps and the issue persists, I would recommend reaching out to IBM support with detailed information about the problem, including any error messages and steps taken to resolve the issue.

input.speaker: user
input.text: What are the different types of SDKs available and why is it necessary for services offering public APIs to also offer SDKs?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: There are three main types of SDKs available for App Configuration: Server-side SDKs, Client-side SDKs, and Admin SDKs. Server-side SDKs are designed for multi-user systems and are intended to be used in trusted environments, such as inside a corporate network or on a web server. Client-side SDKs are meant for use in web and mobile applications and are designed to handle the automatic delivery of the appropriate flag state or property value into your application. The Admin SDK is designed to perform App Configuration service instance management and is used to create and manage configurations for Environments, Collections, Feature flags, Properties, and Segments from within your application. The Server-side SDKs connect to the App Configuration service through a web socket for real-time updates, while the Client-side SDKs pull values from the App Configuration service upon a lifecycle change such as being opened or brought to the foreground. SDKs are available in various client-side and serve-side languages, with more languages being added all the time. It is necessary for services offering public APIs to also offer SDKs because SDKs provide a basic interface that maps directly onto the operations of the underlying API, making it easier for developers to interact with the service. SDKs also handle various tasks like streaming, asynchronous method calls, and instrumentation, allowing developers to focus on building their applications instead of dealing with low-level implementation details.

input.speaker: user
input.text: How can one ensure that the application and SDK continue working even during an App Configuration service downtime?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: To ensure that the application and SDK continue working even during an App Configuration service downtime, you can configure the SDK to work by using a persistent cache. This allows the SDK to store the App Configuration data that is available across your application restarts. When a persistent cache is enabled, the SDK keeps the last known good configuration at the persistent cache. If the App Configuration server is unreachable, the latest configurations at the persistent cache are loaded to the application to continue working. It's important to ensure that the cache file is not lost or deleted, for example, by storing it in a persistent volume by providing the correct absolute path of the persistent directory. Additionally, the SDK is designed to serve configurations and perform feature flag and property evaluations without being connected to the App Configuration service even when offline.

input.text: What are the best practices for using the SDK?

contexts.text:
To aid in adoption of your SDK, you should make your SDKs available in all three major package managers in the Swift network, if they are still useful to your target audience.

Your SDK should follow the [semantic versioning best practices](https://test.cloud.ibm.com/docs/sdk-handbook?topic=sdk-handbook-distributionsemantic-versioning).



 Community support 

Allow your users to find answers to their questions. Users should be able to report problems on GitHub by raising issues on your SDK repository. Having a public Slack channel is a great way to engage with users who have questions specific to their use cases.







 Style guidelines 

You should follow the conventions from [The Official raywenderlich.com Swift Style Guide](https://github.com/raywenderlich/swift-style-guide) and the [Swift API Design Guidelines](https://swift.org/documentation/api-design-guidelines/), with exceptions as follows:



* Use the following style for empty arrays and dictionaries per [The Swift Programming Language](https://docs.swift.org/swift-book/GuidedTour/GuidedTour.html//apple_ref/doc/uid/TP40014097-CH2-ID461l):



let emptyArray = [String]()
let emptyDictionary = [String: Float]()



* Use four spaces for indentation
* Maximum 500 characters line width



For an example SDK with these styles, see the [swift-sdk-template](https://github.ibm.com/CloudEngineering/swift-sdk-template).

Given that the overwhelming majority of SDK adopters will be using Xcode as their development environment, you will want to include your .xcodeproj directory for contributors to easily get started.

You should use the standard [development tools](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/sdk-handbook?topic=sdk-handbook-developer-tools) for Swift to check style and code coverage.





 Standard features 



 Authentication 

You are not required to use a particular library to provide the authentication for your service.

Coding style 



  Coding conventions 

The SDK MUST adhere to coding style that is clearly documented in the SDK CONTRIBUTING file. The SDK SHOULD adopt the coding style recommendation that is given in the appropriate language-specific section of these guidelines.





  Idiomatic style 

The SDK SHOULD employ the generally accepted best practices and common idioms of the specific language. The SDK SHOULD feel "natural" to developers with experience in that language. In particular, the JSON structure of the underlying API request and response structures SHOULD be converted to native language objects with appropriately typed member variables.





  Use style checkers 

The SDK SHOULD employ a code style checker to enforce the established coding style. See [style checkers](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/sdk-handbook?topic=sdk-handbook-devtoolsdevtools-style-checkers).

The releases of these SDKs should be published on [NPM](https://www.npmjs.com/). Your NPM package should be [scoped](https://docs.npmjs.com/creating-and-publishing-scoped-public-packagescreating-a-scoped-public-package) with [@ibm-cloud](https://www.npmjs.com/search?q=%40ibm-cloud), so that NPM users can find similar packages across NPM.

Your SDK should follow the [semantic versioning best practices](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/sdk-handbook?topic=sdk-handbook-distributiondistribution-semver).





 Community support 

Allow your users to find answers to their questions. Users should be able to report problems on GitHub by raising issues on your SDK repository. Having a public Slack channel is a great way to engage with users who have questions specific to their use cases.





 Style guidelines 

You should follow the [Airbnb conventions](https://github.com/airbnb/javascript), with two spaces for indentation.

You should use the standard [development tools](https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/sdk-handbook?topic=sdk-handbook-devtools) for JavaScript to check style and code coverage.





 Streaming support 

If your API takes fileType parameters, you should accept Node.js streams as an input type.





 Dependencies 

Use a well-defined, well-documented request library that includes browser support, like [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios), [superagent](https://github.com/visionmedia/superagent), or [node-fetch](https://github.com/node-fetch/node-fetch).





 Objects for arguments 

The methods in your SDK should take a JSON object as an argument. This object will contain the options for the requests, instead of using positional parameters in the function definition.





 Async architecture

input.speaker: user
input.text: What caused an explosion in the PSL's Equipment Building in 1971?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: The explosion in the PSL's Equipment Building in 1971 was caused by the over-pressurization of compressors due to a butterfly valve between the exhausters and the exhaust stack being left sealed during the midnight shift change. This caused the exhausters to operate like compressors, quickly over-pressurizing the system.

input.speaker: user
input.text: what are the measure to be taken in avoid explosion due to pressurization?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: To prevent explosions caused by over-pressurization, several measures can be taken. Firstly, ensuring that all valves, such as the butterfly valve in this case, are properly opened or closed according to the required procedures. Regular inspections and maintenance of the equipment can also help prevent such incidents. Additionally, incorporating safety devices like check valves into the system can provide an extra layer of protection. Establishing a Recertification Program, as NASA did after the incident, can also ensure that the facility is regularly evaluated for potential risks and that all staff are trained in safe practices.

input.text: Is there a reason to buy a 0% yield bond?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: There might not be a strong reason for an individual to buy a 0% yield bond, as they would likely earn more interest in a low-yield cash account. However, there could be reasons to hold a low-yield (non-zero) bond rather than an alternative low-yield product. For zero-coupon bonds like the EE series US Savings Bonds, it's important to note that while there is no regular interest payment, the bond's value increases over time, and the interest is taxable when the bond is redeemed. The EE series US Savings Bonds also have a special exemption where the interest can be reported as income in the year of redemption instead of each year. But, whether this makes a 0% yield bond a good investment would depend on the individual's financial situation and investment goals.

input.speaker: user
input.text: I keep hearing the term par value of a bond, can you help me understand this, possibly with an example?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: Absolutely, I'd be happy to explain! The par value or face value of a bond is the value of the bond when it's redeemed at maturity. It's the amount of money that the bondholder will receive when the bond reaches its maturity date, assuming they hold the bond until that date. For example, let's consider a bond with a par value of $10,000. If you purchase this bond and hold it until the maturity date specified in the contract, you will receive $10,000. The purchase price, however, is the amount you paid for the bond, and it can be different from the par value. Bonds may sell below, at, or above par. If you paid $9,800 for a bond with a $10,000 par value, you bought the bond below par. A bond selling below par is said to be selling at a discount. Conversely, if you paid $10,200 for the same bond, you bought it above par, and the bond is selling at a premium. If the purchase price and the par value are the same, the bond is selling at par. That means for bonds bought at par (the face value of the bond), the payment it makes is the same as yield. It's important to note that the par value is a fixed amount that doesn't change during the life of the bond, while the purchase price can fluctuate based on various factors such as the bond's creditworthiness, interest rates, and market conditions.

input.speaker: user
input.text: And bond price?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: The bond price is the amount you would pay today to acquire a bond, considering the set of future coupon and principal payments the bond entitles you to. It's determined by the market, just like any other tradeable asset's price. Bond prices may be quoted in various ways, such as a discount or premium relative to the face value or as a premium over a treasury, but ultimately, it should be converted to the total amount you need to pay today. In essence, the bond price represents the present value of the future cash flows the bond promises to pay, taking into account the prevailing interest rates and the bond's specific features, such as its maturity, coupon rate, and face value, which are immutably written into the bond contract. Investors and traders constantly assess and re-assess their valuation of these future cash flows, leading to fluctuations in bond prices as market conditions change. Understanding bond prices and their relationship to the bond's features and market conditions is essential for investors looking to make informed decisions about buying, selling, or holding bonds in their portfolios.

If you pay £1015 for a bond and its current yield is 4.69%, that means you will receive in income each year: 4.69% * £1015 = £47.60 The income from the bond is defined by its coupon rate and its face value, not the market value. So that bond will continue to pay £47.60 each year, regardless of the market price. The market price will go up or down according to the market as a whole, and the credit rating of the issuer.  If the issuer is likely to default, the market price goes down and the yield goes up. If similar companies start offering bonds with higher yields, the market price goes down to make the bond competitive in the market, again raising yield. So if the yield goes up to 4.87%, what is the price such that 4.87% of that price is £47.60? £47.60 / 4.87% = £977.48 Another way to think of it: if the yield goes up from 4.69% to 4.87%, then yield has increased by a factor of: 4.87% / 4.69% = 1.0384 Consequently, market price must decrease by the same factor: £1015 / 1.0384 = £977.48

input.text: one of the causes of the german hyperinflationary period that occurred after world war I was

input.speaker: agent
input.text: The German hyperinflation that occurred after World War I was partly caused by the German central bank (Reichsbank) issuing enormous sums of non-convertible marks to support workers who were on strike against the French occupation of the Ruhr region and to buy foreign currency for reparations. This occurred in 1923 when Germany defaulted on its reparations payments under the Treaty of Versailles. Another factor was that Germany had gone off the gold standard in 1914 and could not effectively return to it due to the loss of its gold reserves due to war reparations. Germans blamed the near - collapse of their economy on the treaty , and some economists estimated that the reparations accounted for as much as one - third of the hyper - inflation .

input.speaker: user
input.text: hyperinflationary

input.speaker: agent
input.text: The term "hyperinflationary" refers to a very high and typically accelerating inflation. In the case of Germany after World War I, this period of hyperinflation was largely caused by the German central bank's (Reichsbank) unlimited printing of notes in response to the devaluation of the mark and the need to support workers on strike and pay reparations. The rate of inflation reached as high as 3.25 × 10 percent per month, with prices doubling every two days. This resulted in the highest denomination of paper money increasing from 50,000 Marks in 1922 to 100,000,000,000,000 Marks in 1923. The hyperinflation was so severe that 1,000,000,000,000 old Marks had to be exchanged for 1 Rentenmark to stabilize the currency.  The inflationary crisis effectively ended in March 1924 with the introduction of the so - called `` gold ruble '' as the country 's standard currency .

input.speaker: user
input.text: Did it lead to any significant political changes?

contexts.text:
Modern history
Europe spent these years rebuilding and coming to terms with the vast human cost of the conflict . The economy of the United States became increasingly intertwined with that of Europe . In Germany , the Weimar Republic gave way to episodes of political and economic turmoil , which culminated with the German hyperinflation of 1923 and the failed Beer Hall Putsch of that same year . When Germany could no longer afford war payments , Wall Street invested heavily in European debts to keep the European economy afloat as a large consumer market for American mass - produced goods . By the middle of the decade , economic development soared in Europe , and the Roaring Twenties broke out in Germany , Britain and France , the second half of the decade becoming known as the `` Golden Twenties '' . In France and francophone Canada , they were also called the `` années folles '' ( `` Crazy Years '' ) .

Gold standard
For example , Germany had gone off the gold standard in 1914 , and could not effectively return to it because War reparations had cost it much of its gold reserves . During the Occupation of the Ruhr the German central bank ( Reichsbank ) issued enormous sums of non-convertible marks to support workers who were on strike against the French occupation and to buy foreign currency for reparations ; this led to the German hyperinflation of the early 1920s and the decimation of the German middle class .

Hyperinflation
Hyperinflation has always been a traumatic experience for the people who suffer it , and the next political regime almost always enacts policies to try to prevent its recurrence . Often this means making the central bank very aggressive about maintaining price stability , as was the case with the German Bundesbank , or moving to some hard basis of currency , such as a currency board . Many governments have enacted extremely stiff wage and price controls in the wake of hyperinflation , but this does not prevent further inflation of the money supply by the central bank , and always leads to widespread shortages of consumer goods if the controls are rigidly enforced .

input.text: How does the query runtime distribution chart differ from the query throughput chart regarding the information displayed?

input.speaker: agent
input.text: The query runtime distribution chart displays the relative duration of queries over time, while the query throughput chart provides a summary of the system's query throughput, showing the number of queries running at any given time. Queries can be grouped based on the frequency of queries per minute or per hour in the query throughput chart. The query runtime distribution chart does not provide information about the number of queries; instead, it shows the duration of individual queries relative to each other over time.

input.speaker: user
input.text: How does the query throughput chart summarize system performance, and what aspects of query activity does it highlight?

input.speaker: agent
input.text:
The query throughput chart summarizes the system’s query throughput by displaying the number of queries running at any given time. It can group queries based on their frequency per minute or per hour, allowing you to observe changes in query activity over time. This information helps identify busy and less busy periods, which can be useful for planning maintenance windows or anticipating potential performance issues.
However, the query throughput chart does not provide details about the duration of individual queries or the overall resource utilization of the system. For this information, you would need to refer to the query runtime distribution chart and the resource consumption chart, respectively.

input.speaker: user
input.text: Why might we need to refer to the query runtime distribution chart and the resource consumption chart?

input.speaker: agent
input.text:
While the query throughput chart provides valuable insights into the number of queries running at any given time, it does not offer information about the duration of individual queries or the overall resource utilization of the system. The query runtime distribution chart addresses this gap by displaying the relative duration of queries over time, giving you a clearer understanding of how long individual queries take to execute.
On the other hand, the resource consumption chart shows overall CPU, disk, fabric network, and memory utilization of the monitored system. This is essential for understanding the system's resource usage patterns and identifying potential resource bottlenecks. By referring to both the query runtime distribution chart and the resource consumption chart, you can gain a more comprehensive view of the system's performance and identify areas that may require optimization or resource allocation adjustments.

input.speaker: agent
input.text: The Star-Spangled Banner was first put to music in 1814 when Thomas Carr of the Carr Music Store in Baltimore published the words and music together under the title "The Star Spangled Banner". However, it was originally called "Defence of Fort M'Henry". Thomas Carr's arrangement introduced the raised fourth which became the standard deviation from "The Anacreontic Song".  In addition , the anthem bicentennial included a youth music celebration including the presentation of the National Anthem Bicentennial Youth Challenge winning composition written by Noah Altshuler .

input.speaker: user
input.text: Did its original composition win any awards?

contexts.text:
The Star-Spangled Banner
Key gave the poem to his brother - in - law Judge Joseph H. Nicholson who saw that the words fit the popular melody `` The Anacreontic Song '' , by English composer John Stafford Smith . This was the official song of the Anacreontic Society , an 18th - century gentlemen 's club of amateur musicians in London . Nicholson took the poem to a printer in Baltimore , who anonymously made the first known broadside printing on September 17 ; of these , two known copies survive .

The Star-Spangled Banner
The poem was set to the tune of a popular British song written by John Stafford Smith for the Anacreontic Society , a men 's social club in London . `` To Anacreon in Heaven '' ( or `` The Anacreontic Song '' ) , with various lyrics , was already popular in the United States . Set to Key 's poem and renamed `` The Star - Spangled Banner '' , it soon became a well - known American patriotic song . With a range of 19 semitones , it is known for being very difficult to sing . Although the poem has four stanzas , only the first is commonly sung today .

On to the Next One
The song won the Grammy Award for Best Rap Performance by a Duo or Group at the 53rd Grammy Awards , making this , in total Jay - Z 's 13th Grammy Award overall , and Swizz Beatz 's first Grammy Award win . In addition , Jay - Z is the first artist to have all four singles from one album win six Grammy Awards in their respective category . The song has been frequently used for freestyles , notably by Ab - Soul , Bun B , Crooked I and Red Cafe . This song was also part of the soundtrack of NBA 2K13 , which was selected by Jay - Z .

input.text: How do I copy the latest version of a design document to a new document?

input.speaker: agent
input.text:
To copy the latest version of a design document to a new document, you can use the COPY request method in HTTP. Here's an example command to copy a design document named "allusers" to a new design document named "copyOfAllusers":
curl "$SERVICE\_URL/users/\_design/allusers" 	-X COPY 	-H "Content-Type: application/json" 	-H "Destination: \_design/copyOfAllusers"
Please note that the IBM Cloudant SDKs currently do not support the HTTP COPY method. Also, copying a design document doesn't automatically reconstruct the view indexes. Like other views, these views are re-created the first time that you access the new view.
The return value of this operation will be the ID and revision of the new document. In the example, it would be something like:
{
"ok": true,
"id": "\_design/copyOfAllusers",
"rev": "1-9c65296036141e575d32ba9c034dd3ee"
}

input.speaker: user
input.text: Did the revision of the new document cause any issues?

contexts.text:
We see why later in the course, but for now, we can assume that the revision number increments with each update to a document.

Let's follow the lifecycle of an example IBM Cloudant document:

When a new document is created (whether with an auto-generated _id or user-supplied _id), it is allocated a revision 1. You are sent the token in the response to your API request. Normally, you can discard the rev (unless you intend to modify the document soon).

When we modify a document whose _rev is at revision 1, the document is saved and a revision 2 token is generated and returned to you in the API response. Notice that we change the name in the document from Liz to Elizabeth.

All simple enough so far.

If we delete the document later, a revision 3 is created.

Unlike almost any other database, IBM Cloudant keeps a reference for deleted documents. A deletion is just another document revision - a special one where _deleted: true replaces the document body.

In fact, the document's recent revision history (the tree of revisions - remember we could have more than one of each revision number) - is kept.

You can't use IBM Cloudant's revision tree as a version control system to retrieve or roll back to an older revision. Once a revision is superseded, the document body of the older revision is deleted, and its disk space is recovered in a process called compaction. Compaction occurs automatically in IBM Cloudant, so it's not safe to assume that old revisions are available to be retrieved.

To summarize, revision tokens are generated by the database on add, edit, and delete. (You never need to create your own revision tokens.) Generally, the revision number increases by one each time, but more complex scenarios are possible (we cover these scenarios later). Older document bodies are discarded or compacted (don't rely on being able to get them back). All IBM Cloudant operations that change a document need the document's _id and its _rev (this scenario is unlike most databases).

That's the end of this part. The next part is called Authentication.







 Authentication video

It offers a virtual cluster of computers available all the time through the Internet, emulating most of the attributes of a real computer including hardware, a choice of operating systems, networking, and pre-loaded application software. AWS's most popular services include Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) and Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3). The offerings are accessed over HTTP, using the REST architectural style and SOAP protocol. AWS comprises more than 90 services spanning a wide range including computing, storage, networking, database, analytics, application services, deployment, management, mobile, developer tools, and tools for the Internet of Things. The technology is implemented at server farms throughout the world and maintained by the Amazon subsidiary. Fees are based on a combination of usage, the hardware/OS/software/networking features chosen by the subscriber, required availability, redundancy, security, and service options. Subscribers can pay for a single virtual AWS computer, a dedicated physical computer, or clusters of either. As part of the subscription agreement, Amazon provides security for subscribers' system. AWS operates from many global geographical regions including 6 in North America.

contexts.text:
Amazon Web Services
Amazon Web Services ( AWS ) is a subsidiary of Amazon.com that provides on - demand cloud computing platforms to individuals , companies and governments , on a paid subscription basis . The technology allows subscribers to have at their disposal a virtual cluster of computers , available all the time , through the Internet . AWS 's version of virtual computers emulate most of the attributes of a real computer including hardware ( CPU ( s ) & GPU ( s ) for processing , local / RAM memory , hard - disk / SSD storage ) ; a choice of operating systems ; networking ; and pre-loaded application software such as web servers , databases , CRM , etc . Each AWS system also virtualizes its console I / O ( keyboard , display , and mouse ) , allowing AWS subscribers to connect to their AWS system using a modern browser . The browser acts as a window into the virtual computer , letting subscribers log - in , configure and use their virtual systems just as they would a real physical computer . They can choose to deploy their AWS systems to provide internet - based services for themselves and their customers .

Client–server model
As microcomputers decreased in price and increased in power from the 1980s to the late 1990s , many organizations transitioned computation from centralized servers , such as mainframes and minicomputers , to fat clients . This afforded greater , more individualized dominion over computer resources , but complicated information technology management . During the 2000s , web applications matured enough to rival application software developed for a specific microarchitecture . This maturation , more affordable mass storage , and the advent of service - oriented architecture were among the factors that gave rise to the cloud computing trend of the 2010s .

Xbox Live
On June 6 , 2011 at E3 , it was announced that the dashboard would be updated again to include the Bing search engine , allowing users to search for games , trailers , movies etc . Kinect would also be updated allowing users to navigate the dashboard and Bing using their voice . Other updates would include cloud storage , YouTube access and live television streaming . The dashboard update was delayed till December 6 , 2011 .

contexts.text:
About Morgan Hill | City of Morgan Hill, CA - Official Website
The nearby Henry W. Coe State Park is a haven for backpackers and nature lovers, offering a truly immersive wilderness experience.Sports Tourism Morgan Hill stands out as a premier destination for sports enthusiasts, owing to its contemporary sports complexes and diverse lodging choices. The City’s outdoor sports facilities in Northern California are unparalleled. The 28-acre Outdoor Sports Center features ten well-maintained grass fields and two illuminated stadium turf fields. At the same time, the Aquatics Center proudly offers an Olympic-size competition pool, two exhilarating water slides, and an instructional pool. This makes Morgan Hill an increasingly popular hub for a wide range of sporting events, from community games to professional tournaments and meets.Thriving Wine IndustryAs the gateway to the Santa Clara Valley wine region, Morgan Hill is a wine lover’s paradise. The City’s wineries and tasting rooms offer a chance to explore a burgeoning wine industry while enjoying beautiful vineyard views.Family-Friendly Morgan Hill is a fantastic place to raise a family. The community values education, with excellent schools and family-friendly activities. Your kids will love the various parks, sports programs, and community events that bring neighborhoods together.Dining Delights Whether you’re craving farm-to-table cuisine, international flavors, or classic American fare, Morgan Hill’s dining scene has it all. Local restaurants and eateries offer a diverse range of culinary experiences, ensuring that every meal is delightful.Festivals and Events Morgan Hill hosts a myriad of annual events and festivals that showcase its community spirit. From the Fourth of July Family Street Dance to Sidewalk Saturday’s Makers Market, there’s always something exciting happening in town.Morgan Hill is a place where the past meets the future, where the traditions of the agricultural heartland harmonize with the innovation of Silicon Valley. It’s a city that celebrates its unique blend of characteristics and welcomes all who seek to be a part of this exceptional community.A Brief HistoryDiana and Hiram Morgan Hill built their estate, the Villa Mira Monte, between the railroad and Monterey Road in 1886. When the first Southern Pacific station was built in 1898, the railroad referred to this area as Huntington. Many visitors would request the train stop at “Morgan Hill’s Ranch,” changing the name to Morgan Hill.
